dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 8 for a seizure call at --Address Redacted-- in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Brandon, an EMT with the rescue squad. What brought you to call 911 today?
Uh, the patient had a seizure about 30 minutes ago while on the couch. We're waiting for the ambulance.
Ma'am, can you open your eyes or respond to voice?
No, her eyes are rolled back, and she is unresponsive.
She has twitching in her hands and feet but no response to voice or touch. She is not moving on her own.
Airway assessment will be performed once she regains some level of consciousness.
Respirations are 16 breaths per minute, regular and unlabored.
SpO2 is 98% while on room air.
Pulse is 112 beats per minute and regular. Blood pressure is 137 over 85.
Her GCS is 6: eyes 1, verbal 1, motor 4. She is postictal and not oriented.
Blood glucose reading is 99 mg/dL.
Skin appears warm, dry, and normal in color. No known rashes or trauma.
Starting four-lead ECG monitoring per protocol for postictal care.
The father reports the seizure started this morning, approximately 30 minutes before the call. How long did the seizure last?
It lasted about a minute. She collapsed after, and she hasn't responded since.
She has a known history of seizures. Does she have any other medical conditions?
She has a history of epilepsy, HIV, and anemia.
Has she taken her Keppra today before this event?
No, the medication was left on the counter. She hadn't taken it.
Pulse has decreased to 70 beats per minute.
Respiratory rate is now 14 breaths per minute.
Blood pressure is measured at 139 over 98 mmHg.
SpO2 is 96% with room air.
EKG confirms she is in normal sinus rhythm.
Performing 12-lead ECG now for further cardiac overview.
Per protocol, we are using Medical-Seizure Protocol (Protocol 3-12). Following standing orders.
Attempting to start IV in the left extremity to ensure circulatory access.
Pulse is now 86, respirations 16, and BP 118/78.
IV attempt has not been successful this moment. No signs of distress observed.
During transport, she became more responsive. Is she now oriented to date, place, and time?
Yes, she opened her eyes and is responding now.
Can she tell us the date and location?
The date is the 8th. I'm at home.
Does she know what happened?
I remember I had a seizure.
Is she experiencing any pain or discomfort right now?
How would you rate the chest pressure now?
About 6 on the scale.
Does the chest pressure spread to any other part of the body?
No, it's just in the middle of my chest.
And what about this pain in your left lower abdomen-on a scale from 0 to 10?
That's a little worse, like an 8.
Does any action or movement make the pain worse?
No, it feels the same no matter what I do.
Noted the reported pain levels. Is there anything else you'd like us to know?
May I check what medications she is currently taking?
She takes Keppra, Zyrtec, Vistaril, and some HIV medications.
What are her known drug allergies, if any?
She is allergic to penicillin, Keflex, Augmentin, and hydrocodone.
What was she doing before the seizure started?
She was sitting on the couch watching TV. She seemed fine before it happened.
She is now alert and oriented to person, place, and time.
VCU Health System has been selected for transport. Is that acceptable to the patient?
Yes, that's the closest one with neurology departments.
Fire and EMS teams are coordinating her transfer from the couch to the stretcher.
Ready to assist with the transfer. Proceed at your signal.
Ready for her removal from the couch. Let us know when to proceed.
She is secured on the stretcher. Prepare for ambulance loading.
EMS stretcher is loaded into the ambulance. She is secured and appears comfortable.
Pulse is now at 86 beats per minute.
Respiratory rate is 16 with SpO2 at 99%.
Blood pressure is 118 over 78 mmHg.
No jowls or jugular venous distention found in the neck.
She is now fully alert and able to speak in full sentences.
She is maintaining normal airway, breathing, and circulation with no signs of NARD.
Notified VCU ED before departure and confirmed readiness for arrival.
Patient is fully alert and oriented with a GCS of 15 now.
Patient transferred from EMS stretcher to hospital cot by patient self-mobility.
Patient signed for transport and care has been officially transferred to the hospital nurse.
She remains stable with pulse 86, respiratory rate 16, and blood pressure 118/78.
She does not require any additional IV attempt at this time.
Pupils are reactive, and no lymphadenopathy is noted in the neck.
No signs of new rashes, bites, or bruising on the skin.
No drainage or bleeding observed in ears or nose.
Her oral cavity is intact with no signs of obstruction.
No signs of deformity, active palpitation, or guarding in chest or abdomen.
She is continuing to respond appropriately and now verbalizes that she is feeling more alert by the minute.
VCU ED has been notified of her current condition and alert status.
I'm still feeling a bit confused, but much better than earlier.
Monitor changes in cognition during transport to ensure no further deterioration is noted.
Patient had one brief episode of NARD following the seizure, resolved before transport.
She is not on any anti-epileptic meds except for Keppra, which was not taken that day.
No signs of pallor or cyanosis, dress is inappropriate to the ambient temperature.
Heart sounds are regular and without murmurs, gallops, or clicks.
Lung sounds are clear and equal bilateral, without wheezing.
Peripheral pulses are present and equal. Capillary refill less than 2 seconds.
She remains conversational and attentive throughout the transport process.
No unusual incidents or changes in vital signs have occurred during transit.
Reassessed the presence of IV access and ensured no signs of infiltration or extravasation.
All vitals remain unchanged; she is stable and comfortable.
VCU ED is aware of the patient's current condition and all necessary pre-arrival information has been transmitted.
She now has full movement in all extremities and currently denies any pain.
She remains alert and oriented to include saying her full name and today's date accurately.
She is verbally acknowledging our movements and is not showing any signs of distress during ambulance motion.
Ambient lighting and exterior noise do not interfere with her condition during transit.
She has provided her current address and is aware the ambulance is on the way to VCU ER.
She is now able to recall three objects after one minute with no prompting.
She is also correctly aware of the season and day of the week.
She is alert, functioning independently, and responding to all inquiries accurately.
Confirmed she has no other acute illnesses or conditions that would complicate hospital treatment.
Contact with the hospital has been maintained and all protocols adhered to before arrival.
The patient is properly prepared for the hospital handoff and remains responsive throughout.
She will be presented at ED with a complete set of vital signs and a full medical history.
The interior of the ambulance remains clean and free of obstructions with medical tools within reach.
The patient is starting to show greater clarity and appears to be recovering well from the seizure.
No further interventions are necessary at this time. Vital signs remain stable during transport.
We continue to monitor for recurrence of seizure but see no signs of one at this time.
Verified current patient status and conditions for ED at 10 minutes before arrival.
The ambulance is at the ED entrance. All necessary equipment remains secured and at the ready.
She is currently verbal, in and out of the hospital, and able to provide her own account of events.
She is not visually impaired and no changes in vital signs require immediate attention or further intervention.
Summarized care following seizure, including initial vitals, medications, and NARD episode during post-ictal state.
She has not regressed back to the postictal state and is now self-mobilizing without help.
Alert throughout the transport, with last verbal response confirming date, time, and location.
Provided full note, including IV attempt results, treatment from seizure protocol, and her recovery during transport.
The hospital nurse received the patient from EMS stretcher and transferred her to the hospital cot independently.
Patient signatures confirm consent and acknowledgment of arrival at VCU for neurological evaluation.
EMS has complied with all standing procedures and ensured patient care was properly transferred to the ED team.
Postictal seizure management performed per protocol. No abnormal detours from the EMS call plan.
The IV attempt in left arm was unsuccessful; this is documented and handed over in the patient care report.
No further interventions required. The patient's vitals remain within appropriate ranges and condition is stable.
EMS transport now fully complete with all notes and records submitted per hospital and municipal protocol.
Final check of equipment and stretcher confirms everything was properly stowed before returning to base.
End of EMS care confirmed by signature and no sign of imminent transport return or further medical emergency.
No outstanding EMS issues to resolve. Patient has been safely transported and care continued with VCU ED.
